name: Iraqi Arabic (Baghdad / gilit)

ancestors.notes: Persian adstrate: extensive loanwords; /p/, /v/, /tʃ/, /ʒ/ phonemes; some phonological calques

notes: Iraqi Arabic — Baghdad Muslim / southern 'gilit' variety (the sedentary koine of Muslim Baghdad, Bedouin-type in origin). The gilit/qeltu split is the defining Mesopotamian isogloss, named after the reflex of the word 'I said': Muslim Baghdad gilit vs Mosul/Jewish/Christian qeltu (Blanc 1964, §2, §3.26). MODELLED FEATURES (cited): (1) DEFINING GILIT REFLEX — OA qaf ق → /ɡ/ (voiced velar; Bedouin origin): JC/qal/ vs M/gal/ 'to say' (Blanc 1964, §3.26, p.26–27; Jasim 2020, §2.4). MSA-learned words keep /q/ (e.g. القرآن), not orthographically separable. (2) GILIT KAF AFFRICATION — kaf ك → [tʃ] in a front-vowel environment (M/čan/ vs JC/kan/ 'he was'; M/čebir/ 'big'), modelled as allophone_rules IQ_GILIT_AFFRIC_K_* conditioned on an adjacent front vowel (Blanc 1964, §3.25, p.25; Jasim 2020, §2.9.2 — /tʃ/ is both a phoneme and an allophone of /k/ in gilit). ENGINE LIMIT: the fully LEXICALISED gilit affrication (M/čalb/ 'dog', where the trigger front vowel is historical) is not orthographically predictable and is NOT captured. (3) qaf-reflex /ɡ/ → [dʒ] affrication before a front vowel (M/geʕad/ ~ [dʒeʕad] 'he sat'), allophone_rules IQ_GILIT_AFFRIC_G_* (Blanc 1964, §3.26 p.28; more regular in rural/Bedouin speech than urban Muslim Baghdad — lexically restricted). (4) INTERDENTALS PRESERVED — ث /θ/, ذ /ð/, ظ /ðˤ/ retained (conservative Mesopotamian feature; Blanc 1964, §3.2; Jasim 2020, §2.4) — unlike Levantine/Egyptian urban merger to /t d/. (5) jim ج → /dʒ/ (retained; inherited from ar-x-mashriqi). (6) EMPHATIC BACKING — low vowels /a aː/ → [ɑ ɑː] adjacent to an emphatic (tafxim; Jasim 2020, the thesis topic), allophone_rules IQ_EMPH_*. Persian adstrate: /p/, /v/, /tʃ/, /ʒ/ established as phonemes; thousands of Persian (and Ottoman-Turkish) loanwords (Blanc 1964, ch.6). INPUT CONTRACT: dialectal Arabic is normally written defectively; short vowels surface only when the input is vocalised (harakat). Erwin (1963) A Short Reference Grammar of Iraqi Arabic is the standard descriptive grammar of gilit Baghdadi but was not obtainable (library/paywall) for this pass; features here are cited from Blanc 1964 and Jasim 2020, both read.

sources.author: Blanc, H.
sources.title: Communal Dialects in Baghdad
sources.publisher: Harvard University Press (Harvard Middle Eastern Monographs X)
sources.notes: Read (OCR full text). §3.25 (p.25) OA/k/: in Muslim (gilit) split into /č/[tʃ] and /k/, JC preserve /k/ — M/čan/, JC/kan/ 'he was'; M/čebir/ 'big'. §3.26 (p.26–27) OA/q/: J and C reflex /q/ in practically every instance, M usually /g/ — JC/qal/, M/gal/ 'to say'; qaf-reflex /g/ affricates to [dʒ] before front vowels (p.28). §3.2 interdentals preserved in Muslim and Jewish. ch.6: Persian and Turkish loan strata; /p/ phoneme.

sources.author: Jasim, M.
sources.title: Tafxiːm in the vowels of Muslawi Qəltu and Baghdadi Gilit dialects of Arabic (PhD thesis)
sources.notes: Read (full PDF). §2.4: /q/ of Muslawi Qəltu is realised as voiced velar [ɡ] in Baghdadi Gilit — the defining split. §2.9.2: in Gilit /tʃ/ is both a separate phoneme and an allophone of /k/; in Muslawi Qəltu /k/ and /tʃ/ are separate phonemes with no allophony. Whole thesis documents tafxim (emphatic backing) of low vowels.

sources.author: Watson, J.C.E.
sources.title: The Phonology and Morphology of Arabic
sources.notes: Secondary reference for the pan-Arabic emphatic/guttural inventory (not the source of the gilit/qeltu claims above).

sources.author: Holes, C.
sources.title: Modern Arabic: Structures, Functions, and Varieties
sources.notes: Secondary reference (not read for this pass).

sources.author: Erwin, W.M.
sources.title: A Short Reference Grammar of Iraqi Arabic
sources.notes: The standard descriptive grammar of gilit Baghdadi. NOT OBTAINED for this pass (library/paywall only) — listed for completeness, no claim here is sourced to it.

allophone_rules.notes: Gilit kaf affrication (Blanc 1964, §3.25 p.25; Jasim 2020, §2.9.2): /k/ → [tʃ] before an adjacent front vowel, e.g. M/čebir/ 'big'. The purely lexicalised residue (M/čan/, M/čalb/, where the trigger vowel is historical) is not orthographically predictable and is not captured.
Gilit kaf affrication (Blanc 1964, §3.25; Jasim 2020, §2.9.2): /k/ → [tʃ] after an adjacent front vowel.

Gilit qaf-reflex affrication (Blanc 1964, §3.26 p.28): the /ɡ/ reflex of OA qaf → [dʒ] before an adjacent front vowel, e.g. M/geʕad/ ~ [dʒeʕad] 'he sat'. Back-vowel words such as M/gal/ 'to say' (back /a/) do not fire, correctly. More regular in rural/Bedouin speech than in urban Muslim Baghdad; the lexicalised residue is not captured.
Gilit qaf-reflex affrication (Blanc 1964, §3.26): the /ɡ/ reflex of OA qaf → [dʒ] after an adjacent front vowel.
Emphatic backing / tafxim (Jasim 2020, the thesis topic; Blanc 1964, §3.3 — [ɑ] near emphatics): short /a/ → [ɑ] adjacent to an emphatic coronal.
Emphatic backing / tafxim (Jasim 2020; Blanc 1964, §3.3): short /a/ → [ɑ] after an emphatic coronal.
Emphatic backing / tafxim (Jasim 2020; Blanc 1964, §3.3): long /aː/ → [ɑː] adjacent to an emphatic coronal.
Emphatic backing / tafxim (Jasim 2020; Blanc 1964, §3.3): long /aː/ → [ɑː] after an emphatic coronal.
